parties: House Foods Group Inc v Community Plant Variety Office
title: CPVO BoA A018/2021 - SK20
text:
Appeal Case A018/2021 
 
DECISION 
of the Board of Appeal of 1 July 2022  
 
  Concerning the a ppeal lodged by 
 House Foods Group Inc   
Osaka, 1-5-7, Mikuriyasakae-machi,  
JP - 577-8520 Higashiosaka-shi, JAPAN 
Representative:   
Meissner Bolte Patentanwälte Rechtsanwälte,  Partnerschaft mbB, Widenmayerstr. 47  
DE - 80538 München, GERMANY  
Represented by Dr. G. Würtenberger  
 
Appellant 
 against a decision of   Community Plant Variety Office 3 boulevard Maréchal Foch 
CS 10121, 
49101 ANGERS CEDEX 2, FRANCE Represented by Ms. O. Lamberti                  
  Defendant     
                                                                                   
   
Relating to Community Plant Variety Right No  EU 58241  
Variety denomination: SK20  
Species: 
Allium cepa  (Cepa Group) 
 
On 1 July 2022, the Board of Appeal of the Community Plant Vari ety Office, composed of Mr. P. van der Kooij 
(Chairman), Mr. R. Brand (Rapporteur) and Ms. V. Melgar (Member ), gave the follo wing decision: 
  1. The appeal is rejected as inadmissible. 
 
2.  The Appellant shall bear the costs of the appeal proceeding s pursuant to Article 85(1) of 
Regulation (EC) No 2100/94. 
   

  
 2 / 11
I            SUMMARY OF FACTS 
 1. The Community Plant Variety Office (hereinafter also CPVO or O ffice) registered the application for a 
Community Plant Variety Right (CPVR) for the onion (
Allium cepa ) variety ‘SK20’ (Applicant House 
Foods Group Inc) on 18 December  2017. The application was given  file No 2017/3314.  
 
The examination of the DUS requirements (distinctness, uniformi ty, stability) was carried out by the 
entrusted Examination Office, Naktuinbouw, located in the Nethe rlands (hereinafter referred to as 
‘Examination Office’, EO or ‘Naktuinbouw’). Plants from seeds h ave been planted in the fields of 
Naktuinbouw in spring 2018 (long day trial) and 2019 (short and  long day trial). Observations ended 
in 2019. 
 2.  The preliminary report was dated 22 January 2018. The Interim  Report, dated 17 October 2018, at the 
e n d  o f  t h e  f i r s t  y e a r  o f  e x a m i n a t i o n ,  d o e s  n o t  m e n t i o n  a n y  i n f o rmation which can affect DUS and 
identification of the variety. It indicates that the bulbs harv ested from long day crop were with small caliber, 
and so the variety was again tested in 2019 under short (sowing  in winter) and long (sowing in spring) 
day period. This report was sent to the applicant on 23 October  2018. 
3.  Naktuinbouw produced the positive Final Report of examination  on 14 September 2020 together with the 
variety description. It concludes that the variety conforms to the DUS requirements. This report was sent 
to the applicant on 21 October 2020. 
4.  Mr. Würtenberger wrote to the CPVO on 21 December 2020: “…Wit h reference to the CPVO's notification 
of 21 October 2020 concerning the draft variety description of the candidate variety ‘SK20’, the Applicant 
requests that the additional characteristic 
[(low lachrymatory factor and pyruvic acid)]  described in Point 
07.02 of the Technical Questionnaire [TQ] is included in the de scription of the characteristics determining 
the scope of protection of the variety. As becomes evident from  the document "Additional Description of 
Variety" attached to the application, the low lachrymatory fact or and pyruvic acid of the candidate variety 
is an outstanding property of the same being of high value for the cultivation and in particular use of the 
candidate variety….” 
5.  Mr. Würtenberger sent to the CPVO, on 10 March 2021, the foll owing publications: 
- a brief communication: “An onion enzyme that makes the eyes w ater” - Nature Vol 419, 17 October 
2002, p.685 (Brief Communications); GS-201 985; 
- a publication in Nature: “Prod uction and characterization of tearless and non-pungent onion” - Scientific 
Reports 623779 DOI 10.1038srep23779.pdf;, published on 6 April 2016. 
He added that the aim of the breeder was to breed a non-lachrym atory variety and requested to take into 
account the additional characteristic. 
 
6. CPVO answered to the applicant on 10 April 2021: 
“Following your letters and technical information to support th e applicant’s request to add in the variety 
description the additional chara cteristic described in Point 07 .02 of the Technical Questionnaire (low 
lachrymatory factor and pyruvic acid), we can confirm that the examination of this additional characteristic 
has not been considered by the Office and that the outcome of t he examination was conclusive on the 
basis of the standard characteristics. 
As mentioned in previous communications, we need to stick to th e CPVO Technical Protocol [TP] and, since 
the characteristic the applicant is asking for is not part of i t, the examination office had no reason to 
observe it during the usual DUS testing. 
In addition, we kindly remind you that the Office does not prov ide a service of testing any characteristic 
mentioned by applicants if there is no such need for the purpos e of the CPVO decision on the application. 
Nevertheless, please be informed that this additional informati on stands in the file of the variety ‘SK20’. 
The CPVO exchanges the technical  questionnaires with the EPO. T hus, if someone else claims a patent for 
the characteristic in question, there will be a trace that ther e is prior art in our documents.” 
 
7.  On 3 May 2021, the CPVO delivered the certificate on the gran t of the CPVR for ‘SK20’ (No: 58241).  

  
 3 / 11
8.  On 1 July 2021, the Appellant filed a notice of appeal, the r eceipt of which was acknowledged on 5 
July 2021. House Foods Group Inc, represented by Mr. Würtenberg er, produced an “Appeal against 
latter decision and request that the additional characteristic described in point 7.02 of the technical 
questionnaire, namely the low la chrymatory factor and pyruvic a cid, is included in the description of 
the characteristics determining the scope of the protection of the variety,  
in the alternative,  that the technical examination will be reissued to examine the candidate variety with regard to that 
characteristic”.  
 
9. The Board of Appeal registry acknowledged receipt, on 5 July 20 21, of the appeal contesting:  
- the decision to grant CPVR EU 58241, 
- and non-compliance of a reques t filed by the a pplicant under point 7.2 of the technical questionnaire. 
 
10. On 25 August 2021,  Mr. Würtenberger produce d the grounds of appeal. 
               In summary, the following argume nts were mentioned. Regulation  2100/94 (hereinafter also Basic   
 Regulation or BR), in Article 6, does not define which charact eristics establishing distinctness have 
 to be taken into consideration within the examination. 
Thus the regulation set up general conditions and allows to tak e into account the advance in breeding 
new varieties. The CPVO is bound to observe in the application of the Basic Regulation the principles 
set up by the Convention and the guidelines based on the UPOV C onvention. It should therefore be 
possible to add characteristics to the ones mentioned in the Te chnical protocol, if they are useful 
provided they meet the conditions set out in the general introd uction to DUS examination (TG 1/3, 
4.2.3). 
 
The variety ‘SK20’ shows such additional characteristic:  
- the amount of lachrymatory factor and pyruvic acid are very l ow, 
- the tear inducing property and pungency are absent or very we ak. 
The characteristic described is a property of the variety being  of high value for the cultivation and use 
of the variety and is a remarkable progress in onion breeding. The additional characteristic was not examined by the EO, nor d id the President of the Office consider 
whether this characteristic coul d be a characteristic which was  able to make the candidate variety more 
distinct. The additional characteristic might fulfill the general require ments laid down in TG 1/3 (4.2) and Art. 
5 (2) BR. 
 
  It would have been the duty of the Office to take into consider ation the additional characteristic. 
Pursuant to point III.6 of the Technical protocol and Art. 83(3 ) BR an applicant may claim, either in 
t h e  T Q  o r  d u r i n g  t h e  t e s t ,  t h a t  a  c a n d i d a t e  v a r i e t y  h a s  a  c h a r a cteristic which would be useful in 
establishing distinctness. If such claim is made, and is suppor ted by reliable technical information, a 
special test may be undertaken providing that a technical accep table test procedure can be drafted.  
The applicant has the right that  all those characteristics whic h made the variety distinct are taken into 
account. If this is not done, this will be to the (commercial) detriment of the applicant. The scope of 
protection of the candidate variety in so far is limited. The CPVO decision to grant the C PVR does not insert any conside ration for the claim of the applicant.  
Thus neither the Office, nor the President have complied with t heir duty to properly examine all the 
facts of the application for CPVR with care to exercise the dis cretion provided by the TP. 
 
11. On 16 September 2021, an interlocutory revision of the appeal w a s  c a r r i e d  o u t  b y  t h e  R e c t i f i c a t i o n  
Committee of the CPVO. It decided not to rectify the contested decision, for the reasons put forward below, 
in summary.  
On the admissibility of the appeal 
The Appellant is in essence asking for an amendment of the vari ety description in the sense that the 
additional characteristic ‘low l achrymatory factor and pyruvic acid’ should be included in the description of 
characteristics determining the scope of protection of the vari ety. 
In that connection, the appeal es sentially aims at claiming tha t since the applicant had requested an 
additional characteristic, the Office and its President had the  d u t y  t o  a s s e s s  a n d  a d d  i t  t o  t h e  l i s t  o f 

  
 4 / 11
characteristics, since this characteristic has a high value for  cultivation and, in particular, for the use of the 
variety, and would make the variety itself “even more distinct from other known varieties”. As regards the 
subject matter of the appeal, it is clear that the Appellant do es not challenge the decision to grant a CPVR 
to the variety ‘SK20’ but part of the variety description regar ding the list of characteristics. 
Under the above circumstances, the Office contends that the App ellant does not have standing to appeal 
to the Board of Appeal. As a general principle of procedural law that the Office shall apply pursuant to Article 81 of the Basic 
Regulation the legitimate interest in pursuing an appeal is den ied if the requested appeal does not remove 
in any case the contested decision. In the current case, the ap peal is not even directed against the decision 
to grant a CPVR but to a part of the variety description. 
Furthermore, the losing party principle is based both on the ef fects of a decision and on their aptitude to 
affect the party concerned which is not the case here as the de cision to grant a CPVR for ‘SK20’ has not 
b e e n  c h a l l e n g e d .  N o r  c a n  t h e  c l a i m s  p u t  f o r w a r d  i n  r e l a t i o n  t o  the relevance that the additional 
characteristic plays in terms of market use of the variety, be of relevance at the time of assessing whether 
a candidate variety satisfies the DUS criteria pursuant to the applicable Technical Protocol. As it is evident 
f r o m  t h e  C P V R  l e g a l  f r a m e w o r k ,  v a l u e  f o r  c u l t i v a t i o n  a n d  u s e  ( V CU) or marketing/competitive 
considerations fall outside of the criteria to assess the suita bility of a characteristic with a view to grant a 
CPVR. 
The Board of Appeal, in case No A006/2014 of 29 April 2016 (‘Ta ng Gold’), declared the appeal inadmissible 
in the part in which the Appella nt asked an amendment of the va riety description with the inclusion of a 
number of extra characteristics. The Board of Appeal concluded,  “The granting authority has the 
discretionary power to decide wh ich characteristics establish t he requested clear distinctness between the 
candidate variety and the varieties of common knowledge”. 
The Committee considers that, mutatis mutandis, the same ratio decidendi of case No A006/2014 as 
regards admissibility of the appeal to seek an amendment of par t of the variety description of a decision 
to grant CPVR equally applies to the case at hand. 
For all of the above reasons, the Committee considered that the  appeal shall be dec lared inadmissible 
pursuant to Article 49(1) of the Proceedings Regulation (herein after also PR). 
 
On the substance 
For the sake of completeness , the Committee put forward the following arguments on the substa nce of 
the appeal.  
- 
On the conduct of the technical examination  
U n d e r  A r t i c l e  5 6 ( 2 )  o f  t h e  B a s i c  R e g u l a t i o n ,  t h e  c o n d u c t  o f  a n y  technical examinations is to be in 
accordance with test guidelines issued by the Administrative Co uncil and any instructions given by the 
Office. The applicable CPVO Technical Protocol is CPVO-TP/046/2 of 1 Ap ril 2009. Under section I it is established 
that the protocol describes the technical procedures to be foll owed in order to meet the Council Regulation 
2100/94 on Community Plant Variety Rights. The technical proced ures have been agreed by the 
Administrative Council and are based on general UPOV Document T G 1/3 and UPOV Guideline TG/046/7 
dated 09/04/2008 for the conduct of tests for Distinctness, Uni formity and Stability. This protocol applies 
to varieties of Allium cepa (Cepa Group). 
Under section III (conduct of tests), paragraph 3 (characterist ics to be used), it is established that the 
“characteristics to be used in DUS tests and preparation of des criptions shall be those referred to in the 
Annex 1. All the characteristics shall be used. (..) The Admini strative Council empowers the President, in 
accordance with Article 23 of Commission Regulation N° 1239/95,  to insert additional characteristics and 
their expressions in respect of a variety.” 
Under section III, paragraph 6 (S pecial tests), it is establish ed “In accordance with Article 83(3) of Council 
Regulation No. 2100/94 an applicant may claim either in the Tec hnical Questionnaire or during the test 
that a candidate has a characteristic which would be helpful in  establishing distinctness. If such a claim is 
made and is supported by reliable technical data, a special tes t may be undertaken providing that a 
technically acceptable test procedure can be devised.” It also says that “Special tests will be undertaken, 

  
 5 / 11
with the agreement of the President of CPVO, where distinctness  i s  u n l i k e l y  t o  b e  s h o w n  u s i n g  t h e  
characters listed in the protocol.” 
Finally, under section III, paragraph 7.a) (Distinctness), it i s established “A candidate variety will be 
considered to be distinct if it meets the requirements of Artic le 7 of Council Regulation No. 2100/94.” 
The General Court has stated that the protocols and test guidel i n e s ,  w h i c h  a r e  a d o p t e d  b y  t h e  
Administrative Council acting on a proposal from the President of the CPVO, “are akin to legal rules which 
the CPVO has imposed on itself in order to ensure that technica l examinations are properly conducted 
under conditions objectively established in advance and that th e CPVO is therefore required, in principle, 
to comply with them in ongoing procedures before it”. 
In view of the above, the Committee concluded that,  
First, the technical examination shall be conducted according t o the rules established in the technical 
protocol as approved b y the Administrative Council, to meet the  requirements of the Basic Regulation. 
Second, the characteristics to be used in DUS tests and prepara tion of descriptions shall be those referred 
to in Annex 1 to the technical protocol. Third, the applicant may claim in  the Technical Questionnaire o r during the test that a candidate has a 
characteristic, which would be h elpful in establishing distinct ness. This criterion has a supplementary value 
in respect to the compulsory list of characteristics that the A dministrative Council of the Office has adopted. 
Therefore, any such additional characteristics would be relied upon only in case where it would be helpful 
of establishing distinctness and provided that a technically ac ceptable test procedure can be devised. 
Fourth, those special tests will be undertaken only where disti nctness is unlikely to be shown relying on 
the characteristics listed in the applicable protocol. 
Fifth, distinctness shall be assessed under the requirements of  Article 7 of the Basic Regulation. 
It should also be pointed out that a variety shall be distingui shable from any other plant grouping by the 
expression of at least one chara cteristic (Article 5(2) Basic R egulation). Therefore, in the assessment of 
distinctness, the Office shall not verify whether a candidate v ariety is “even more” distinct from varieties 
of common knowledge, but to comply with the applicable legal re quirements. In the case at hand, the 
variety ‘SK20’ was declared distinct from the varieties of comm on knowledge. Due to its unique 
morphology, in particular in respect of the characteristics fol iage waxiness (No. 3), bulb shape (No. 18), 
skin colour (No. 23) and adherence of dry skin (No. 21) no simi lar variety had been identified amongst the 
comparison varieties during the examination. 
Therefore, the Office was not obliged to request the Examinatio n  O f f i c e  t o  o r g a n i z e  s p e c i a l  t e s t s  a s  
requested by the Appellant. It follows from all the foregoin g considerations that in the pr esent case, the technical examination of the 
variety ‘SK20’ has been conducted in compliance with the above rules pursuant to the applicable protocol. 
No technical matters have been reported by the Examination Offi ce in declaring distinctness on the basis 
of the list of characteristics of Annex 1 to the protocol. Ther efore, pursuant to the a pplicable protocol, the 
Office did not resort to the said additional characteristic. 
 
- 
On the power of the President of the CPVO to authorize the use of an additional characteristic  
In the first place, pursuant to Article 22(1) of the Proceeding s Regulation, the Administrative Council takes 
a decision as to the test guidelines upon proposal of the Presi dent of the CPVO. Article 22(2) of that 
regulation specifies that, in the absence of a decision of the Administrative Council as to test guidelines, 
the President of the CPVO may take a provisional decision there on. Moreover, under Article 23(1) of the 
Proceedings Regulation, where th e Administrative Council takes a decision on test guidelines, it shall 
include a power whereby the President of the CPVO may insert ad ditional characteristics and their 
expressions in respect of a variety. 
The Court of Justice of the EU (CJEU) stated [See judgment of 8  June 2017 in Schniga GmbH v. CPVO, 
case C-625/15P (‘Gala Schnitzer’), ECLI:EU:C:2017:435, paragrap hs 54 and 55] that “As the Advocate 
General noted, in point 97 of his Opinion, the assessment of th e characteristics of a plant variety necessarily 
contains a particular uncertaint y due to the nature of the obje ct itself to which the technical examination 
relates, namely a plant variety, as well as the length of time required to conduct such an examination”. 
Furthermore, “In those circumstances, only the flexibility that  allows the recognised power of the President 
of the CPVO, under Article 23(1) of the implementing regulation , to insert additional characteristics in 
respect of a variety is also capable of ensuring the objectivit y of the procedure for granting Community 

  
 6 / 11
plant variety rights. Thus, an application for a Community plan t variety right may not be rejected solely on 
the ground that the characteristic of an examined variety, foun d during the technical examination and 
decisive for assessing the distinctiveness of that variety in c omparison with other varieties, was not referred 
to in either the technical quest ionnaire completed by the appli cant or in the relevant test guidelines and 
protocols.”  
It follows from the foregoing that the President of the Office exercises a discretionary power when deciding 
whether to agree to the use of a proposed additional characteri stic and that said flexibility is also capable 
of ensuring the objectivity of the procedure for granting Commu nity plant variety rights, as confirmed by 
the CJEU in the above-referenced judgment. 
Accordingly, the Office and the President did not breach the ap plicable rules as regard s the conduct of the 
technical examination of  the variety ‘SK20’. 
 
- On the suspensory ef fect of the appeal  
The Appellant has not requested the Office that pursuant to Art icle 67(2) of the Basic Regulation, the 
contested decision not be suspended as a result of the appeal. 
In view of the above considerations, the rectification of the c ontested decision was not granted. 
 
12. On 22 September 2021, the Appellant was informed of the decisi on of the Rectification Committee. 
The case was remitted to  the Bo ard o f Appeal to  examine the app eal, pursuant to Article 71 Council 
Regulation (EC) No 2100/94. 
13.     The hearing in this case before the Board of Appeal was he ld on 20 April 2022 as virtual hearing by 
Video Conference. The statements  of the parties to proceedings are summarized below. 
 13.1  The Appellant recall ed principles justifying the grant of an exclusive right to plant breeding, making reference 
to the Basic Regulation: 
- the purpose of granting a CPVR is to support the investment and  the resulting achievement of this 
investment in order to give incentives to breeders to invest in to further competitive products; 
- in order to stimulate the breeding and the development of new v arieties, there should be improved 
protection compared with  the current situation; 
- for the purpose of granting a CPVR, an assessment of important characteristics relating to the variety 
is necessary; however, these characteristics do not necessarily  need to be related to their economic 
importance.  
For the Appellant, the logic behind these principles, in partic ular if there are important characteristics of 
economic value, is that these ch aracteristics should be taken i nto consideration in the technical examination 
of the candidate variety for granting a CPVR. 
 13.2 For the Appellant, th e arguments of the CPVO, in relation to t he non-admissibility of the appeal, disregarded 
what should be protected by a CPVR: it is the candidate variety  with its specific characteristics to be included 
in the description of the characteristics as it determines the scope of the protection. In Article 5(2) BR 
d e f i n i n g  t h e  o b j e c t  o f  a  C P V R ,  i t  i s  m e n t i o n e d  t h a t  a n y  c h a r a c t eristic allowing to differentiate the new 
breeding results so as to establish whether a candidate variety  is distinct, uniform and stable are to be taken 
into account in examination proceedings.  
The Appellant complained that in the granting proceedings, neit her the CPVO nor the examination office has 
mentioned why the outstanding characteristic has not been taken  into consideration, although the same had 
been mentioned already in the technical questionnaire. 
 
13.3  The Appellant wished to come back to a basic principle contai ned in the UPOV Convention which is that the 
o n l y  b i n d i n g  o b l i g a t i o n s  o n  U P O V  m e m b e r s  a r e  t h o s e  c o n t a i n e d  i n  t h e  U P O V  C o n v e n t i o n .  G e n e r a l  
i n t r o d u c t i o n  a n d  t e c h n i c a l  p r o t o c o l s  p r o v i d e  g e n e r a l  g u i d a n c e .  The purpose of the relevant technical 
guidelines is to determine a minimum of characteristics to be c onsidered in every case and not to exclude 
additional characteristics which fulfil the DUS requirements. T he characteristics included in the test guidelines 

  
 7 / 11
are not necessarily exhaustive a nd must be extended with additi onal characteristics if proved to allow to 
determine distinctness, stability or uniformity.   As per the admissibility of the appeal, the Appellant recalled that the CPVO relied on the Board of Appeal 
decision of appeal case A006/2014 ‘Tang Gold’. The Appellant ar gued that the CPVO disregarded that the 
grant of a protection is the confirmation that the candidate va riety fulfils the DUS requirements.  
To the Appellant, the CPVO took a false decision because the gr anted protection does not correspond to the 
application and to the candidate  variety description.  The curr ent variety description limits the scope of 
protection as it does not include all the characteristics inclu ded in the application of the candidate variety.  
The applicant is burdened with a  deficiency of the decision and  as such, lodging an appeal on the grant of 
protection is justified and admissible. The protection turns ou t its value from the variety description and since 
the additional characteristic is not included, the scope of pro tection is limited and does not acknowledge the 
achievements of the applicant. 
 
13.4 W i t h  r e g a r d  t o  s u b s t a n c e s ,  t h e  t a b l e  o f  c h a r a c t e r i s t i c s  i n  t h e  technical protocol CPVO TP/46/2 is not 
exhaustive for the reasons mentioned above and should not have prevented the examination office to include 
the examination of the additional characteristic declared by th e applicant. 
In this context, the Appellant referred to two basic principles  the CPVO is subject to: 
- ‘sound administration’ in accordance with which the CPVO must  consider, to a reasonable extent, 
factors which are in support or against a protection applied fo r, which includes really important new 
characteristics, 
-  ‘to give statement of reasons for granting’, which is entire ly missing in this case, as no reason has been 
given to explain why this additional characteristic was not inc luded when granting the CPVR.  
 
13.5 The CPVO replied  with the arguments div ided in four main part s: first, the admis sibility of this appeal; then 
two points of substances which are the assessment of distinctne ss established on the basis of the technical 
protocol and the power of the Pr esident to consider additional characteristics; finally, the reply to other 
arguments raised in the grounds of appeal. 
 
13.6  
Admissibility 
The main argument of the CPVO was that this appeal should be co nsidered as inadmissible pursuant to 
Article 49(1) PR together with Article 81 BR.  Article 49(1) PR  states that if the appeal does not comply with 
the provisions of the BR, it should be considered inadmissible.  Article 81 refers to the basic principle of 
procedural law which has to be applied by the CPVO. In this cas e, the CPVO considered that the Appellant 
does not have a locus standi  because he does not have an interest in pursuing an appeal. Th erefore, the 
Appellant does not qualify as a losing party as the right was g ranted. The interest in pursuing an appeal is 
denied if the requested appeal does not remove in any case the contested decision. In this appeal, the 
Appellant does not request to  annul the grant decision.  
 The CPVO also referred to recent case-law, in particular case C -281/18 P, in which it is settled that for a 
person to have an interest in br inging appeal proceedings, the appeal must be capable, if successful, of 
procuring an advantage to that p arty.  In this case, the CPVO d id not consider that a successful appeal would 
result in giving the advantage that the Appellant seeks.   
 
Contrary to the Appellant, the C PVO considered that the Board o f Appeal decision in case ‘Tang Gold’ 
(A006/2014) applies mutatis mutandis to this case and is releva nt, because in said case, the Appellant also 
requested additional characteristics to be added to the variety  description. In this decision, the Board of 
Appeal considered that the grant ing authority has the discretio nary power to decide w hich characteristics 
establish the requested clear distinctness between the candidat e variety and the varieties of common 
knowledge. Contrary to what the Appellant is claiming, the CPVO  had no obligation to take into consideration 
any additional characteristic.  
 

  
 8 / 11
When mentioning the innovative value of the additional characte ristic, the Appellant makes reference to a 
criterion which is external to PVR domain, as the potential mar ket value of the variety (value for cultivation 
and use or VCU) is not among the criteria to establish the fulf ilment of the DUS requirements, proper of the 
marketing authorization system of certain species.   
 
13.7 Substance  
 13.7.1 
The distinctness shall be established on the basis of the techn ical protocol  
The first point in substance is the fact that the distinctness shall be established on the basis of the technical 
protocol. Article 55 BR states that where the CPVO has not disc overed any impediment to the grant of a 
Community plant variety right, the CPVO has to organise a techn ical examination to assess that the DUS 
requirements are satisfied. The concept of distinctness is expl ained in Articles 5 and 7 BR: the candidate 
variety should be clearly distin guishable from any other plant variety of common knowledge by at least one 
characteristic. Consequently, the distinctness observed in one characteristic is sufficient to grant a right . 
Therefore, the argument of the Appellant that the additional ch aracteristic would make the variety ‘even 
more distinct’ cannot be taken into consideration as the distin ctness criterion was already satisfied. As to the 
conduct of the technical examination (Article 56 of the BR), th e CPVO recalled that the test guidelines or 
technical protocol used are considered as binding rules (see jo int General Court Judgements T-91/14 and 
92/14). In this case, the technical protocol applicable was CPV O-TP/046/2 of 2009 in which the organisation 
o f the technical examinatio n is set,  and in particular,  in whic h  it  i s  i n dic at ed  t ha t  s pe ci al  te st s  s h al l b e 
undertaken, with the agreement of  the President of CPVO, where distinctness is unlikely to be shown using 
the characteristics listed in the protocol. In this case, the c andidate variety was already morphologically so 
distinct from varieties of common knowledge that the examinatio n office could not identify a reference variety 
for comparison. So, there was no need to invoke the additional characteristic procedure.  
Furthermore, this approach was not only consistent with the law  but also with the instructions at UPOV level: 
paragraph 4.2.3 of TG 1/3, also mentioned by the Appellant, set s out that the characteristics included in the 
individual test guidelines are not necessarily exhaustive and m ay be expanded with additional characteristics 
if that proves to be useful. The CPVO considered that it has di scretionary power to establish whether to use 
additional characteristics or not, and in this case, given that  the variety was fully distinct, there was no need 
for an additional characteristic . This was also consistent with  what is written in the CPVO internal procedure 
on additional characteristics. The distinctness was established  on the basis of multiple morphological 
characteristics, all listed in Annex 1 of the technical protoco l.  
 
13.7.2  The power of the President to consider additional characteristi cs 
T h e  s e c o n d  p o i n t  i n  s u b s t a n c e  i s  t h e  p o w e r  o f  t h e  P r e s i d e n t  t o  authorise the use of an additional 
characteristic. According to the Appellant, the CPVO President had the duty to take a decision on the 
additional characteristic once this is suggested in the Technic al Questionnaire. The argument of the CPVO is 
that there is no duty for the President to invoke the additiona l characteristic procedure when there is no 
need.  The CPVO referred to Judgment C-625/15P and Judgement C- 534/10P which indeed specify the 
discretion of the CPVO given by the scientific nature of the wo rk carried out by the CPVO.   The Proceedings 
Regulation (Articles 22 and 23) is clear in establishing that a  decision of the President on additional 
characteristics is an option (no t an obligation) to be exercise d upon discretion. Moreover, the discretion of 
the President cannot be an arbitrary power and has still to com ply with the binding rules of the technical 
protocol which establishes the use of additional characteristic s only when there is an issue in establishing 
distinctness. 
 
13.8 The CPVO replied to the other arguments of the Appellant.   
The fact that the additional characteristic was indicated in th e technical protocol (Section 07.02 indicates 
“ a d d i t i o n a l  c h a r a c t e r i s t i c s  w h i c h  m a y  h e l p  t o  d i s t i n g u i s h  t h e  v ariety”) does not establish rights for the 
applicant as the technical protocol submitted pursuant to Artic le 50(1)(f) BR has only indicative value. It does 
not oblige the CPVO to take additional characteristics into con sideration. 

  
 9 / 11
Regarding Article 83(3) BR which , according to the Appellant, a llows the applicant to request the examination 
of additional characteristics, t he CPVO disagreed as the purpos e of this article is to pr ovide the possibility to 
carry out extra tests on the candidate variety by the examinati on office, with subsequent fees to be borne 
by the applicant, when this is needed for the grant of a CPVR, which was not the case for ‘SK20’, as the right 
was granted.  The principle of sound administration and 
ex officio  obligation mentioned by the Appellant are not contested 
b y  t h e  C P V O ,  h o w e v e r  t h e  C P V O  c o n s i d e r e d  t o  h a v e  t a k e n  a n  i n f o r med decision. There were many 
exchanges with the examination office in which the technical ex pert requested clarifications on the reference 
variety, and on whether this additional characteristic was rele vant. The need to invoke the additional 
characteristic was not necessary even if the test was uncomplic ated and inexpensive as argued by the 
Appellant. Finally, regarding the argument of the Appellant that the decis ion does not indicate whether the President 
exercised its power concerning additional characteristics nor w hy the latter were not taken into account, that 
the CPVO decision lacked motivation, the CPVO disagreed and sta ted that it has no need to provide precise 
reasoning when granting a plant variety right. When the CPVO gr ants a right, the legal basis is quoted, the 
result of the final report is pr ovided as well as the variety d escription. Moreover, in this case, the CPVO 
communicated with the applicant before granting the title on wh y the additional characteristic was not taken 
into consideration.      
  
13.9  The Appellant highlig hted the following. 
- The Appellant had the general impression that the CPVO regards the grant of a plant variety right as a purely 
administrative act; it neither considers the interest of the br eeder of the candidate variety nor the public 
interest. The Appellant recalled that the purpose of an exclusi ve right is to foster breeding activities to create 
new varieties that wil l at the end serve the public (considerat ions 5 and 11 of the BR).  
- Concerning admissibility, the Appellant disagreed on the argume nt of the CPVO stating that the applicant has 
no legal interest in this appeal: the applicant has not only an  interest but a right to get a granted right which 
has the best extent of protection within the frame given by the  BR and important characteristics are an 
essential part of this gain.  The Appellant agreed that commerc ial value is not decisive unless the DUS 
requirements are not fulfilled. The applicant has a legitimate and legal interest in the appeal because the 
applicant did not get what he was asking for, namely a descript ion of the variety representing the candidate 
variety. Referring to Art.1.vi. of UPOV Convention, the Appella nt recalled that a variety is defined by “the 
expression of the characteristics resulting from a given genoty pe or combination of genotypes, distinguished 
from any other plant grouping by the expression of at least one  of the said characteristics…”.  The full 
description of the characteristics of the candidate variety sho uld indeed be part of the granted right. Just 
obtaining protection has no value on the market without this sp ecific characteristic.  
- The Appellant strongly disagreed on the CPVO argument about the  intention of the Appellant to overextend 
the scope of protection. The Appellant argued that the IP right  has the purpose to give the applicant the best 
possible extent of protection for his achievements.  
- The technical protocols are of c ourse binding rules but set up as minimum standards in order to guarantee 
equal treatment, which do not ex clude the possibility of the CP VO and its President to, at least, consider the 
request of the applicant to take into consideration an addition al characteristic. The CPVO argument, that the 
President would have considered additional characteristics only  if one of the DUS criteria was not fulfilled, 
disregards the purpose of an exclusive right which is to observ e characteristics which deserve protection. As 
the 
Schniga  case shows, the President has the discretion to carefully deci de on additional characteristics to 
be taken into account. Protocols are not binding with regard to  additional characteristics but binding regarding 
a certain level of characteristi cs which have to be examined. T he technical protocols show the flexibility of 
the system as they provide the possibility to use other charact eristics in case a certain characteristic cannot 
be examined. This is necessary to really satisfy the purpose of  plant breeding. 
- The ex officio  examination obliges the CPVO to take additional characteristic s into account, or at least to 
explain why it has not considered it in the examination of the candidate variety. 

  
 10 / 11 
 
13.10  The CPVO highlighted the following. 
 
- The CPVO considered that it respected the binding provisions an d general principles. It reminded the 
timeline of the application process: once the CPVO received the  final report from the examination office, 
two months were given to the app licant to comment. The issue of  the additional characteristic was raised 
by the applicant on the last day for comment and assessed by th e CPVO. In fact, this issue had already 
been assessed during the examination of the candidate variety b y  t h e  C P V O  w h i c h  c o n s i d e r e d  n o t  
necessary to invoke this additional characteristic. The CPVO, a fter an internal consultation, replied to the 
applicant in April 2021 why this additional characteristic was not examined.  
- The CPVO does not have the obligation to reply to every argumen t of the Appellant, did assess if this 
additional characteristic was useful and concluded that it was not necessary as the candidate variety was 
already distinct.  
- To tackle the issue of the public interest, the CPVO indeed ser ves the public at large and applies equal 
treatment which means applying the same rules to every applican t. In this case, however, a private interest 
and a special treatment are discussed.  
- Finally, the CPVO recalled that the characteristics on which th e title is granted are morphological and do 
not necessarily have a commercial value. 
 
 II         REASONS   A.  On the admissibility of the appeal  
 
14. The appeal is inadmissible pursuant to Article 49(1) of Regula tion (EC) No 874/2009 in conjunction with 
Article 81 of Regulation (EC) 2100/94.  
 
Pursuant to Article 49(1) PR in conjunction with Article 81 BR the legitimate interest in pursuing an appeal 
is denied if the requested appeal does not remove in any case t he contested decision, which is the case 
here, as the Appellant does not challenge the decision to grant  a CPVR to the variety ‘SK20’, but only part 
of the variety description regarding the list of characteristic s.  
The losing party principle is based both on the effects of a de cision and on their aptitude to affect the party 
concerned which is not the case here as the decision to grant a  CPVR for ‘SK20’ has not been challenged.  
   
B.  On the substance 
 
15       For the sake of precedence and completeness it is, in spite of the inadmissibility of the appeal, worthwhile 
to consider its substance.  
 15.1 
The assessment of distinctness established on the basis of the technical protocol   
 
The distinctness shall be established on the basis of the techn ical protocol (see point 13.7.2). The candidate 
variety should be clearly distin guishable from any other plant variety of common knowledge by at least one 
characteristic (Art. 5 and 7 BR), which was the case here.  Therefore, the argument of the Appellant that the 
additional characteristic would make the variety ‘(even more) d istinct’ cannot be taken into consideration as 
the distinctness criterion was already satisfied. There was no need to invoke the additional characteristic 
procedure.  Furthermore, this approach was also consistent with the instruc tions at UPOV level: paragraph 4.2.3 of TG 
1/3 sets out that the characteristics included in the individua l test guidelines are not necessarily exhaustive 

  
 11 / 11 
and may be expanded with additional characteristics if that pro ves to be useful. The CPVO has discretionary 
power to establish whether to use additional characteristics or  not, and in this case, given that the variety 
was fully distinct, there was no need for an additional charact eristic. This was also consistent with what is 
written in the CPVO internal procedure. The distinctness was es tablished on the basis of multiple 
morphological characteristics, all listed in Annex 1 of the tec hnical protocol.  
15.2   The power of the President to consider additional characteristi cs 
  
When an additional characteristi c is suggested by an applicant,  there is no obligation for the President of the 
Office to invoke the procedure laid down in Articles 22 and 23 PR when there is no need to do so, in particular 
once the distinctness of the variety has already been establish ed.    
This principle is also reflected by Judgments C-625/15P and  C- 534/10P which  specify the discretion of the 
CPVO given by the scientific nature of the work carried out by it.   
 
 
  
III      FINAL CONCLUSION   16.      The Board of Appeal does not find decisive arguments in t he appeal for repealing the decision of the CPVO 
to grant a CPVR to the variety ‘SK20’ given that the technical examination was conducted pursuant to the 
Technical Protocol. 
          These arguments must therefore be rejected in their e ntirety. 
  
IV       ON THE COSTS 
  17.     Since the appeal is rejected as inadmissible the Appellant  shall bear the costs of the appeal proceedings 
pursuant to Article 85 (1) BR. 
              V          ON LEGAL REMEDIES   18.       Pursuant to Article 73 BR, an action may be brought bef ore the Court of Justice of the European Union 
            against this decision within two months of its serv ice.  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Paul VAN DER KOOIJ       Richard BRAND   
 Chairman of the Board of Appeal     Rapporteur
